An excerpt from a long discussion that caught my eye:

"As I mentioned in the introduction, I am a very strong advocate of decanting virtually
every example of pinot noir or red Burgundy that I drink and believe that there are few red wine
grape varieties that benefit more from proper aeration than pinot noir. … Whenever I read “popped and poured” for pinot noirs, whether young or old, as part of an amateur’s tasting note, I simply cringe.

I totally agree - do you?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: when I pop and pour, it’s into a decanter. To me it just means an anti-Audouze.

posts.name: T 0 m Altmayer
posts.cooked: I decant virtually every red wine and many whites. I find the biggest improvement from decanting on the aromatics, but certainly there is a benefit on the palate as well.

I decant virtually every red wine and many whites. I find the biggest improvement from decanting on the aromatics, but certainly there is a benefit on the palate as well.

I am not very interested in catching every drop of the wine at its most open. The story of the wine, how it develops, is much more interesting and exciting to me. I respect John very much, but on the subject of decanting, this amateur is not particularly intimidated by his judgment.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
An excerpt from a long discussion that caught my eye:

"As I mentioned in the introduction, I am a very strong advocate of decanting virtually
every example of pinot noir or red Burgundy that I drink and believe that there are few red wine
grape varieties that benefit more from proper aeration than pinot noir. … Whenever I read “popped and poured” for pinot noirs, whether young or old, as part of an amateur’s tasting note, I simply cringe.

Yup. I think it’s a waste not to decant Northern Rhones and nebbiolo, too. Most red wines, actually, unless they’re quite old and delicate, and a lot of whites.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
I take a more cautious approach, trying a little pour first: if it’s fantastic, why take a chance on decanting making it “even better”?

This might work with younger wines, but if you have an older bottle that you’ve stood up in order to let the sediment settle down then you’ve lost that as soon as you tip the bottle to make that first pour.

Speaking of decanting, this is one of the things that drives me batty about a lot of wine lovers. They’ll age a bottle for 25 years to where they think it’s ready to drink, then they will pull it from their cellar, throw it in the car, and drive it to a dinner or tasting and then decant it without giving it time to stand up and without any thought for the texture of the wine. Yeah, it still might taste good, but one of the reasons we age wine is to smooth out the texture and create suppleness. You totally lose this if you travel with an older wine without decanting it first. Most wines that have been cellared need some air, so stand it up for a week, and decant it properly.

posts.name: Tom DeBiase
posts.cooked:
Decant just about everything even though I might forget to mention it when writing a tasting note

Tom
I’d love to hijack this thread just a tiny bit and ask about old Sangiovese. anyone have a special procedure for 20+ year old Brunello or chianti?

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Old nebbiolo and wines I know from experience need air are exceptions. But then, I don’t find red wine improved overall on Day 2, with few exceptions.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Intimidation has nothing to do with my agreement with him.

You have a lot of experience, I have no criticism whatsoever. But I think a lot of people decant wine because it makes them feel like serious wine people. And it imbues the wine with significance. Many blind tastings show people prefer wine pop and pour. So who can say? There are plenty of wines I feel improve with a little air. There are wines that most definitely shut down or fail. I choose my default on the cautious side. You can always give more air, but you can never take it back.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: I do taste first, and if fine, then no decant; but I almost always find myself decanting older reds and usually whites.

Totally agree with this. I try not to open good wines unless I have at least a solid hour or two to sit with them and experience their development in the glass

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
I do not decant unless the wine seems like it needs it.

Frankly, I don’t want more crap to wash.

I rarely decant wine except for sediment and even then I just usually stand it up before serving so as to keep the sediment in the bottom of the bottle.
As to another point above, I can count the number of red table wines I have had that improved the second day it was open on one hand. And have 5 fingers left.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: Always an interesting discussion. I happen to be a decanter (often a double decant). But I would ask the reverse question. Give some examples of wines that have been hurt by decanting (not just given too much air, but the act of pouring into another vessel), and explain why this occurs. There are a substantial number of excellent wine people who are opposed to decanting because they feel it diminishes the wine (If I get this straight they feel that it can suppress the nose and impact some of the more delicate and subtle aspects of the wine.) This includes most Burgundians, and a number of Piedmont winemakers. Personally I don’t really understand it and haven’t experienced it (although I’m not sure how I would be able to tell in some controlled manner). I am open minded, however, and despite having 40 or so years of experience, willing to consider that I’m wrong.

I rarely decant, but more for practical reasons. I get home from work (well, whenever that happens again) about 20 minutes before dinner, which is, on a good day, a 20 minute affair. Even if I’m going to a dinner the best I can do is a short double decant in the morning (or overnight but that’s a bit risky) before taking my bottle to work.

I decant older Burgs to get them off their sediment.

I decant younger Burgs to aerate.

I rarely decant whites and have never decanted Champagne.

I don’t know what is right but that has worked for me.

What do you mean by old or oldish? I’ve never experienced that with nebbiolo from the 50’s, 60’s or 70’s. Those are almost always much better with a ton of air, though not always decanted. Old nebbiolo is the exception to most rules. What I’d consider on the young side - say 90,s on - then yes, those definitely shut down so I do not decant them.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Intimidation has nothing to do with my agreement with him.

You have a lot of experience, I have no criticism whatsoever. But I think a lot of people decant wine because it makes them feel like serious wine people. And it imbues the wine with significance. Many blind tastings show people prefer wine pop and pour. So who can say? There are plenty of wines I feel improve with a little air. There are wines that most definitely shut down or fail. need to give this a hefty cosign, especially the last part. i have found extended decanting to lock up red burgundy more often than not.

When most of us talk about decanting we’re not talking about decanting, then locking up somewhere for 6 hours. We’re talking about decanting, and then doing what you would do with a non-decanted bottle. checking now and then, drinking slowly. perhaps serving at dinner an hour or two or three later. The only time where I see extended decanting locking up a wine being a problem is the situation where someone brings a wine to dinner that they decanted 8 hours ahead of time…

I don’t have a TON of experience with old wines, but I have had a couple of times with oldish to old Nebbiolo where I opened it, and the first initial whiffs from the bottle are BEAUTIFUL. then for some reason even with a ton of airtime (8+ hours) it just never gets back to that initial whiff. the same thing happens when im pouring them into the decanter. anyone else ever experience this?

What do you mean by old or oldish? I’ve never experienced that with nebbiolo from the 50’s, 60’s or 70’s. Those are almost always much better with a ton of air, though not always decanted. Old nebbiolo is the exception to most rules. What I’d consider on the young side - say 90,s on - then yes, those definitely shut down so I do not decant them.

the two most notable ones were an 82 produttori normale and a 99 Giacosa Barbaresco. both of them I opened, smelled (didn’t taste) double decanted back into bottle about 3-4 hours later, and then started drinking about 1-2 hours after that (over a couple hours).

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked:
Old red burgundy needs decanting off its sediment, though that is not as important as allowing a long time for that sediment to settle after transport, which is in turn not as important as allowing the temperature to rise above 15C/59F during the process.
It seems to me that with younger red burgundies decanting is slightly more likely to close rather than open the wines.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
I don’t have a TON of experience with old wines, but I have had a couple of times with oldish to old Nebbiolo where I opened it, and the first initial whiffs from the bottle are BEAUTIFUL. then for some reason even with a ton of airtime (8+ hours) it just never gets back to that initial whiff. the same thing happens when im pouring them into the decanter. anyone else ever experience this?

What do you mean by old or oldish? I’ve never experienced that with nebbiolo from the 50’s, 60’s or 70’s. Those are almost always much better with a ton of air, though not always decanted. Old nebbiolo is the exception to most rules. What I’d consider on the young side - say 90,s on - then yes, those definitely shut down so I do not decant them.

the two most notable ones were an 82 produttori normale and a 99 Giacosa Barbaresco. both of them I opened, smelled (didn’t taste) double decanted back into bottle about 3-4 hours later, and then started drinking about 1-2 hours after that (over a couple hours).

That makes more sense. A '99 Giacosa is still a young nebbiolo, to me, and should be treated differently - for me, no decant because they shut down just as you describe. Nebbiolo from the '80s is generally on the cusp for me, and whether I treat it as young or old depends on my experience with the producer and the wine; so for an '82 Produttori normale, I would not expect it to act like a young wine (shut down), but I also wouldn’t think it would need an extended decant. Wine can always surprise you, though.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
I would love to hear more thoughts on decanting older Nebbiolo (1975, 1985). I have a bias for a longer decant for most wines, but feel out of my depth with some of these older, potentially more fragile wines.

Jen - there are several long threads that specifically discuss this subject within the last year. A bit of work with the search function should get you there.

Every bottle is its own (longer or shorter) time capsule. To make a rule that covers all is simply nonsense.

Some young wines beg a heavy hand to remove their carbon dioxide - in fact quite a lot do and I don’t see them tightening afterwards - whereas some old wines can light up a room yet are dead within 10 minutes of contact to oxygen.

I decant if there’s a specific reason - such as removing hundreds of small bits of cork from one that’s disintegrated - a much more important problem than whether to decant, or in what kind of decanter is required. I also like to watch a wine develop in the glass - and if I don’t have time to follow it - I’ll open a fresh and fruity Chablis or Beaujolais-Villages instead…

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Every bottle is its own (longer or shorter) time capsule. To make a rule that covers all is simply nonsense.

Couldn’t agree more, which is why I usually say my default is to be cautious and not decant unless I have reason to do so. Reasons include getting off sediment, knowing the wine has benefited from it in the past, knowing the producer’s wines often benefit from extra air, familiarity with the category (old nebbiolo), among others.

I think it’s useful to have a place to begin - for me, not decanting - and then depart from there when it makes sense. And it’s totally accurate to look back and say that historically more often than not I don’t end up decanting.

Thanks for the opportunity to clarify.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Well, I know that I dislike Gilman’s view of “amateur,”

I think John is using “amateur” in the French sense of the word, i.e. an enthusiast, without its pejorative Anglophone connotations of inexperience.

I think this is clear. “Amateur” is used to contrast not only with “professional,” but also to contrast with someone who enjoys wine but doesn’t make it a priority, or put the time and effort into understanding and enjoying it that a true amateur does. Unless they are ITB, calling someone a true amateur is a compliment.

I should have added “as Gilman is using it” after saying it’s a compliment. It certainly seems more common in the American vernacular for “Joe Blow is such an amateur” to be intended, and taken, as an insult to Joe Blow’s expertise and experience, and thus it’s understandable that one would read it that way, but I think it’s clear JG is using it here in the original/French sense.

Cheers!

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
In general agree with the sip / strategise approach, the big question is when to to take that first sip relative to the dinner timing (having neither a Coravin nor a TARDIS )
Someone mentioned whites. I quite often find myself decanting younger Chardonnays to open them out, Or simply opening well ahead of time; meal logistics often dictate that letting the wine evolve over the evening won’t work as I’m not a fan of tasting whites after reds.

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked:
I almost never decant unless the wine is young and I know that it was made in a more reductive fashion. If I know that a wine has sediment, then I’ll plan ahead and leave it upright for days and pour carefully.
I even remember a time when the rule was that Burgs and Rhone wines should never be decanted even for sediment. The sloped shoulders clearly also make this difficult. Instead you should have one of those cute baskets where the wine can lie at a slight angle and be poured from the basket.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
What do you mean by old or oldish? I’ve never experienced that with nebbiolo from the 50’s, 60’s or 70’s. Those are almost always much better with a ton of air, though not always decanted. Old nebbiolo is the exception to most rules. What I’d consider on the young side - say 90,s on - then yes, those definitely shut down so I do not decant them.

the two most notable ones were an 82 produttori normale and a 99 Giacosa Barbaresco. both of them I opened, smelled (didn’t taste) double decanted back into bottle about 3-4 hours later, and then started drinking about 1-2 hours after that (over a couple hours).

That makes more sense. A '99 Giacosa is still a young nebbiolo, to me, and should be treated differently - for me, no decant because they shut down just as you describe. Nebbiolo from the '80s is generally on the cusp for me, and whether I treat it as young or old depends on my experience with the producer and the wine; so for an '82 Produttori normale, I would not expect it to act like a young wine (shut down), but I also wouldn’t think it would need an extended decant. Wine can always surprise you, though.

For older Nebbiolos (wine by wine as to what is old but I generally agree with your thoughts on that), I stand up for weeks and decant (or double decant) off sediment right before or shortly before (< 1 hr if going to a restaurant) drinking. I think it’s critical to decant off their fine sediment. But I’ve also had many experiences where older decanted Barolo and Barbaresco open and close and open again numerous times even during the course of a dinner. They have tremendous evolution with air that a 6-8-hour decant will likely miss entirely. I’m guessing that’s what happened with the 82 Produttori and 99 Giacosa here. (99 Giacosa is definitely young - agree there too. [Added: it could just shut down.])

posts.name: Mike Francisco
posts.cooked:
Sarah K, I think your thoughts at the beginning of this tread are spot on. The stuff about 40, 50 & beyond year old Nebbiolo, I will leave to those with far more experience with them, then I.

On a bit of an aside, I am always amazed how people turn things like wine, something for fun and relaxation, into a job. Not the people in the industry, this is their job. You must have the right glasses, decanters, do everything just so, or it’s just ruined. I find it all a little exhausting.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
On a bit of an aside, I am always amazed how people turn things like wine, something for fun and relaxation, into a job. Not the people in the industry, this is their job. You must have the right glasses, decanters, do everything just so, or it’s just ruined. I find it all a little exhausting.

That’s one view. Another is that it’s part of the fun. Something like thinking about what wine to bring to a fun Barolo tasting planned 2 months out and standing it up or decanting for a dinner with friends is part of that fun. To give a nod to what came to mind — a great foodie movie, Ratatouille — it’s about perspective.

I’ll give it to you that being snobby about glassware is a bore, but part of the fun is also to discover that the sensory experience depends on the delivery vessel. It’s part of the geekdom that many embrace. Personally, I’ve collapsed to drinking most wines out of the same glass, Gabriel Gold, which are the only ones I’ve bought since getting a boatload of Riedel Vinums of every sort as wedding gifts 20 years ago. Love my GGGs!
